coredata.dc:title: A novel optical sensor based on carbon dots embedded molecularly imprinted silica for selective acetamiprid detection
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Poshteh Shirani, Marziyeh
Rezaei, Behzad
Ensafi, Ali A.
coredata.dc:description: A molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) on silane-doped carbon dots (Si-CDs) has been synthesized as a novel optical sensor for selective detection of acetamiprid (ACT). Highly fluorescence Si-CDs have been first prepared by hydrothermal method. Subsequently, MIP has been formed on the surface of Si-CDs (MIP@Si-CDs) by a sol-gel process. The properties and structure of the Si-CDs and MIP@Si-CD nanocomposites have been characterized by fluorescence spectroscopy, UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy, dynamic light scattering, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, and X-ray diffraction techniques. Various parameters, which affect the optical signal, have been optimized. The fluorescence signal of MIP@Si-CDs showed linear response with ACT concentration in the 7–107 nM range with a detection limit of 2 nM and a precision of 2.7% and 3.3% for five replicate detections of 17 and 33 nM ACT under optimal conditions. In addition, the sensor has been satisfactorily utilized for the detection of ACT content in real samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Acetamiprid
Silane-doped carbon dots
Optical sensor
Molecularly imprinted polymer
Sol-gel method

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of MIP@Si-CDs and NIP@Si-CDs Nanocomposites

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) UV–Vis spectrum of the Si-CDs, (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of the Si-CDs at different excitation wavelengths, (C) FT-IR of Si-CDs.
(A) XRD diagram of Si-CDs, (B) TEM image of Si-CDs and (C) DLS analysis of the Si-CDs.
TEM image of (A) MIP@Si-CDs, (B) NIP@Si-CDs and (C) FT-IR spectra of MIP@Si-CDs and NIP@Si-CDs.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of NIP@Si-CDs (black line), MIP@Si-CDs before and after template extraction (blue line and red line), (B) UV–Vis spectra of ACT and MIP@Si-CDs, (C) UV–Vis spectrum of ACT and fluorescence spectrum of MIP@Si-CDs.

(A) Effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity of MIP@Si-CDs, Conditions: 0.5 mg mL−1 of CQDs-MIPs, 55 nM of ACT at different pH (in the range of 3–10), (B) Effect of temperature on the fluorescence intensity of MIP@Si-CDs. Conditions: 0.5 mg mL−1 of MIP@Si-CDs, 55 nM of ACT at pH 7.0.

(A) Effect of the amount of MIP@Si-CDs on the fluorescence intensity of MIP@Si-CDs. Conditions: 55 nM of ACT, at pH 7.0 with different amount of MIP@Si-CDs and at 25 °C. (B) Effect of time on the fluorescence intensity of MIP@Si-CDs; Conditions: 2 mg mL−1 of MIP@Si-CDs, 55 nM of ACT at pH 7.0 and at 25 °C.
(A) Effects of time on the MIP@Si-CDs fluorescence intensity; (B) Effect of ionic strength on the MIP@Si-CDs fluorescence intensity; (C) Recovery efficiency of MIP@Si-CDs.

Fluorescence spectra of (A) MIP@Si-CDs, (B) NIP@Si-CDs with increased ACT concentration (which are 7, 11, 22, 33, 44, 55, 65, 76, 86, 97 and 107), The Stern−Volmer plots for (C) MIP@Si-CDs and (D) NIP@Si-CDs with different concentrations of ACT at the optimum conditions.
The fluorescence responses of MIP@Si-CDs, NIP@Si-CDs and Si-CDs to different species (each of the concentrations was 5.5 μM).
Comparison of different methods for the quantification of ACT.
Results of recovery analysis of ACT in different samples.
The statistical results for comparison of the present method with the HPLC for the determination of ACT in water and apple samples.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The pooled value of the standard deviation.

head.ce:title: A novel optical sensor based on carbon dots embedded molecularly imprinted silica for selective acetamiprid detection

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Isfahan University of Technology, Isfahan 84156-83111, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) on silane-doped carbon dots (Si-CDs) has been synthesized as a novel optical sensor for selective detection of acetamiprid (ACT). Highly fluorescence Si-CDs have been first prepared by hydrothermal method. Subsequently, MIP has been formed on the surface of Si-CDs (MIP@Si-CDs) by a sol-gel process. The properties and structure of the Si-CDs and MIP@Si-CD nanocomposites have been characterized by fluorescence spectroscopy, UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy, dynamic light scattering, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, and X-ray diffraction techniques. Various parameters, which affect the optical signal, have been optimized. The fluorescence signal of MIP@Si-CDs showed linear response with ACT concentration in the 7–107 nM range with a detection limit of 2 nM and a precision of 2.7% and 3.3% for five replicate detections of 17 and 33 nM ACT under optimal conditions. In addition, the sensor has been satisfactorily utilized for the detection of ACT content in real samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Acetamiprid (ACT) is a systemic broad-spectrum insecticide of neonicotinoid type [1]. It has been extensively applied as a substitute for organophosphorus and other conventional insecticides to control sucking type insects in different crops given [1,2]. ACT is potentially dangerous to humans though it is not classified as a human carcinogen. ACT has recently been reported to cause erectile dysfunction in human males [3]. Therefore, it is very important to expand an efficient and dependable analytical strategy to determine ACT residues in environment and food. The present methods for determination of ACT residues consist of different liquid and gas chromatographic methods [4,5], and their coupling with mass spectrometry [6,7]. These methods require time consuming sample preparation, expensive equipment, and skilled operators though they are highly sensitive and accurate. The Enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [8] as a fast and simple method has also been employed in the determination of residual ACT. This method is simple and fast but is sensitive to organic solvent interferences and matrix constituent. Consequently, it is desirable to expand a simpler and more efficient method for selective and rapid determination of ACT in the fields of environment and food safety. A smart method for obtaining three-dimensional crosslinked polymers with fit built recognition sites is molecular imprinting technique [9]. Polymerization of functional monomers and crosslinkers in the attendance of the template molecules is used to prepare molecularly imprinted polymers (MIP) [10]. Binding sites are exposed, allowing the binding of the target molecule with very high specificity and affinity after the removal of the template [11]. Sol-gel polymerization has been more attractive among different MIP preparation methods due to its distinct benefits such as simplicity, environmentally friendly synthesis and mild polymerization conditions [12].

Carbon dots (CDs) are the modern luminescent carbon nanomaterial family members [13]. CDs are remarkably significant given their high chemical inertness, bright fluorescence, easy functionalization, low toxicity and high solubility compared with semiconductor quantum dots and conventional dyes [14,15]. CDs can be promptly used in bioimaging, [16] catalysis [17] and sensing due to their prominent characteristics [18].

A label-free and simple fluorescent approach has been proposed for sensing ACT based on MIP coated Si-CD nanocomposites in this work. Citric acid and 3‑aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES) have been reacted by the hydrothermal method to form organosilane functionalized carbon dots (Si-CDs). MIPs have then been deposited at the surface of Si-CDs through sol-gel method. ACT, APTES, and tetramethoxysilane (TEOS) have been used as the template, functional monomer, and crosslinker, respectively, in the synthesis of MIPs. The morphology and fluorescent characteristics of the synthetic MIP@Si-CDs have been studied. The present approach has prominent advantages such as high selectivity, high sensitivity, label-free and facile synthesis in comparison with conventional methods. MIP@Si-CDs have been applied as a sensor for ACT detection in the real samples.

Triton X-100, 3‑aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES), tetraethoxysilane (TEOS), cyclohexane, ammonia solution (25–28%), acetone, acetonitrile, and ethanol were obtained from Sigma Aldrich Chemical Co. (USA). Acetamiprid (N‑[(6‑chloropyridin‑3‑yl)methyl]‑N′‑cyano‑N‑methylethanimidamide) was kindly provided by the Aria Shimi Co. (Zahedan, Iran). Analytical grade chemicals and reagents and doubly distilled water were used throughout the experiments. Universal buffer solution prepared using phosphoric acid, boric acid, and acetic acid (0.04 M) and the pH was adjusted with 0.05 M NaOH solution.

A Jasco FP–750 spectrofluorimeter (Japan) was used to record the fluorescence spectra. A Jasco V-570 UV–Vis/NIR spectrophotometer (Japan) was used to record the absorption spectra. A Jasco 680-plus FT-IR spectrophotometer (Japan) was applied to obtain Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra in 4000–400 cm−1 range at a resolution of 4 cm−1. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was applied to examine the structure and size distribution of the Si-CDs and the prepared nanocomposites using a Philips CM 120 instrument (Netherlands). The particle dimension distribution of Si-CDs was found using a Malvern ZEN3600 dynamic light scattering (DLS) instrument (UK). X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was performed using an Asenware AW-XDM300 (China).

Si-CDs were synthesized as previously reported by Chen and et al. [19]. APTES (500 μL) and citric acid (1.0 g) dissolved in deionized water (15 mL) and then transferred into a 30 mL stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 4 h. Afterward, the autoclave was cooled to ambient temperature at the end of the reaction. The colorless solution changed to pale yellow color at this stage. A 3.5 kDa dialysis bag was utilized for 24 h to remove unreacted starting materials and the as-prepared Si-CDs solution was collected and kept at 4 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of MIP@Si-CDs and NIP@Si-CDs Nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: Reverse microemulsion technique was applied for ACT imprinting in silica nanosphere embedded Si-CDs [20]. Triton X-100 (1.8 mL) was dissolved in cyclohexane (7.5 mL) under vigorous magnetic stirring over a period of 15 min. Si-CDs (400 μL), TEOS (50 μL) and ammonium hydroxide (100 μL) were added consecutively and the solution was stirred for 2 h. Subsequently, it was mixed with APTES (20 μL) and ACT (5.0 mg) and the reaction system was sealed overnight at ambient temperature under continuous stirring. The microemulsion was finally broken by acetone (20 mL) and the nanocomposites were collected by centrifugation (12,000 rpm for 5 min). Having discarded the supernatant, the unreacted reagents and template molecules (ACT) were removed using a solvent mixture of ethanol/acetonitrile (v/v, 8:2) until no template (ACT) was found by UV–Vis spectrometry. The purified MIP@Si-CDs were dried in vacuo at 50 °C. Furthermore, the non-imprinted particles (NIP@Si-CDs) were used as a control in the evaluation of the molecular recognition properties of imprinted materials prepared under above conditions in the absence of ACT.

MIP@Si-CDs (6.0 mg) was dispersed in the pH 7.0 universal buffer (3.0 mL) by ultrasonication. A certain concentration of ACT solution was then introduced to the above mixture. Fluorescence measurements were performed at the excitation wavelength of 340 nm and the emission signal was recorded between 365–600 nm. The fluorescence intensity was recorded in the absence and presence of ACT and referred to as F0 and F, respectively. The F0/F value was used as a response signal to assess ACT concentration.

Wastewater and apple samples were selected to examine the capability of MIP@Si-CDs in ACT detection. To remove the suspended particles, wastewater was filtered by a 0.22 μm microporous membrane. As well, apples were first homogenized to a free-flowing puree in a mortar and were then centrifuged for 10 min at 6000 rpm to eliminate the particulate matter. The recovery study was performed to determine ACT in real matrix samples by the standard addition method. Each test was conducted in triplicate.

Si-CDs have been prepared using citric acid and APTES by hydrothermal method. A strong absorption band in the UV–Vis spectra at 334 nm (Fig. 1(A)), which is associated with the n-π* transition of CO and π-π* transition of CC bonds present at the surface of Si-CDs [21]. Based on the fluorescence spectra of the Si-CDs (Fig. 1(B)), the maximum of fluorescence emission intensity is obtained with an excitation wavelength of 340 nm. Thus, this wavelength was selected as an excitation wavelength for all experiments. The vibrational bands at 3380 and 3150 cm−1 in the FT-IR spectrum of Si-CDs (Fig. 1(C)) are ascribed to the corresponding OH and NH, respectively. The bands around 2920 and 1360 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibration of CH and the peaks in 1700 and 1560 cm−1 are associated with the stretching vibrations of CO and CC, respectively. The peak around 1100 cm−1 is due to the stretching vibration of Si-O. XRD patterns were used to identify the crystalline nature of Si-CDs. The two diffraction peaks at 2θ = 20.05 and 44.05° are associated with the graphite lattice spacings of (002) and (101), respectively, (Fig. 2(A)), further confirming the crystalline graphite like structure of the synthetic Si-CDs [22]. CDs have a uniform diameter of 3.3 ± 0.4 nm on the basis of the TEM image (Fig. 2(B)). The hydrodynamic size was estimated as 3.1 ± 0.5 nm using DLS test (Fig. 2(C)).

Fig. 3(A and B) displays the TEM images of MIP@Si-CDs and NIP@Si-CDs. The comparison of the TEM images of NIP@Si-CDs (as the blank) and MIP@Si-CDs shows that the presence of ACT in the formation of the nanostructures does not disrupt or cause aggregation. Fig. 3(C) indicates the FT-IR spectra of MIP@Si-CDs and NIP@Si-CDs. The broad peak at 1000–1100 cm−1 corresponds to the asymmetric stretching vibrations of SiOSi and the bands around 425 and 730 cm−1 are associated with the SiO asymmetric stretching vibration. The peaks at approximately 3350 and 1560 cm−1 are due to the stretching vibration of NH and absorption bonds around 2880 and 1380 cm−1 are associated with the CH stretching and bending vibrations, respectively.

The sol-gel method was selected in order to prepare MIP on the surface of Si-CDs. MIP@Si-CDs were synthesized using ACT, TEOS, and APTES as template molecule, cross linker and functional monomer, respectively, and NH3·H2O as the catalyst. Si-CDs were coated with layered silica prior to MIP formation to improve the interaction between Si-CDs and imprinted polymers [23]. Silica layer was formed on the surface of Si-CDs by TEOS hydrolysis catalyzed by ammonium hydroxide. MIPs were prepared by copolymerization of APTES and TEOS in the presence of ACT template. Si-CDs and APTES interacted with ACT via hydrogen bonding and Van der Waals force. MIP@Si-CDs nanostructures with particular ACT recognition sites were created following the removal of template molecules and unreacted reagents through washing with ethanol and acetonitrile. The fluorescence spectra of MIP@Si-CDs were recorded prior to and following the removal of ACT molecules (template). Fig. 4(A) indicates that the s fluorescence signal of MIP@Si-CDs was 14% of NIP@Si-CDs before removing the template, but a 93% intensity was recovered after removal. The results indicated the effective removal of the ACT from MIP@Si-CDs. The fluorescence quenching mechanism may involve energy or charge transfer from MIP@Si-CDs to ACT [24,25]. As observed by the UV–Vis absorption spectra (Fig. 4(B)), the UV absorption spectrum of the ACT was close to the band gap of the MIP@Si-CDs. However, the UV–Vis absorption peak of the ACT was away from the emission peak of the MIP@Si-CDs (Fig. 4(C)). The result indicated a +35 (mV) zeta potential for MIP@Si-CDs. The zeta potential stayed almost constant upon mixing with the ACT. The charges in the MIP@Si-CDs conduction bands may be transferred to the ACT lowest unoccupied molecular orbital [26]. Energy transfer was ruled out as a possible mechanism for the fluorescence quenching since there were not any spectral overlaps between the absorption spectra of the ACT and the emission spectra of the MIP@Si-CDs. The fluorescence quenching mechanism is due to the charge transfer from the MIP@Si-CDs to ACT, according to these results.

The effect of pH on the fluorescence signal of MIP@Si-CDs nanocomposite containing 55 nM ACT was investigated in the 2–10 range. Maximum quenching of the fluorescence signal was observed at pH 7.0, according to Fig. 5(A). Thus, pH 7.0 was selected as the optimal pH for all experiments.

To obtain the highest sensor sensitivity for the determination of ACT, the effect of the amount of MIP@Si-CDs (0.5–2.5 mg mL−1) was investigated at pH 7.0. The responses were followed in the presence of 55 nM ACT at ambient temperature. The highest sensitivity was obtained when 2 mg mL−1 of MIP@Si-CDs was used (Fig. 5(B)). Reducing the amount of MIP@Si-CDs resulted in lower sensitivity since the signal to noise ratio reduced. Furthermore, the sensitivity decreased when MIP@Si-CDs amounts of over 2 mg mL−1 were used and self-absorption of the fluorescence occurred. Thus, 2 mg mL−1 was used as the optimal amount of the MIP@Si-CDs.

The temperature effect (25–45 °C) on the MIP@Si-CDs fluorescence signal to ACT (55 nM) has been investigated at pH 7.0. The fluorescence signal of the MIP@Si-CDs declined with increasing temperature, as observed in Fig. 6(A). Thus, the study was further performed at ambient temperature.

A significant parameter, which affects the fluorescent intensity and the interaction between the analyte and recognition cavities, is the time interval between the addition of ACT and fluorescence measurement. The response time of MIP@Si-CDs in the presence of 55 nM ACT at ambient temperature and pH 7.0 has been studied. As observed in Fig. 6(B), the response time increased up to 120 s and then stayed almost unchanged. Therefore, 120 s was used as a suitable response time.

The stability of the synthesized MIP@Si-CDs in a universal buffer solution (pH = 7.0) was studied at ambient temperature by consecutive measurements of the fluorescence intensity over time. The fluorescence intensity reduced to less than 3.0% after 6 h (Fig. 7(A)). The stability of MIP@Si-CDs solid powder has also been studied. No significant changes were observed in the fluorescence intensity after 5 months. Various NaCl concentrations were used to test the influence of ionic strength on the MIP@Si-CDs fluorescence, as observed in Fig. 7(B). The fluorescence intensity of the MIP@Si-CDs was found to remain constant in the presence of different ionic strengths.

A 2 mg mL−1 MIP@Si-CDs solution containing 55 nM ACT at pH 7.0 was used to investigate the effect of the sensor recovery. For this purpose, the fluorescence intensity (F0/F) and following %recovery were calculated. The MIP@Si-CDs were recovered by centrifuging and washed with ethanol/acetonitrile (8:2 v/v). The MIP@Si-CDs were studied one more time with ACT under the same conditions. The recovery efficiency of the sensor remained almost constant after 5 times, according to Fig. 7(C) but remarkably decreased afterward. As a result, this sensor is capable of being used up to 5 times.

The fluorescence spectra of MIP@Si-CDs and NIP@Si-CDs were recorded in the different concentrations of ACT to compare the recognition abilities of MIP@Si-CDs and NIP@Si-CDs. As observed in Fig. 8(A and B), MIP@Si-CDs is more sensitive than NIP@Si-CDs, which is due to the imprinted ACT on the MIP@Si-CDs surface. The Stern-Volmer equation can be used to describe the relationship between the response signal and ACT concentration as a quencher [27]:
F 0
/ F = 1 + KsvCACT
where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities before and after addition ACT, respectively, KSV is the ACT Stern-Volmer quenching constant and CACT is the ACT concentration. Fig. 8(C and D) shows the Stern-Volmer plot.

MIP@Si-CDs showed linear response to ACT in the 7–107 nM range with calibration equation of F0/F = 0.0138CACT + 0.9412 (R2 = 0.9915), the limit of detection (LOD) being 2 nM. A relative standard deviation (RSD) of 2.7% and 3.3% were calculated by five repeated analyses of 17 and 33 nM solutions, respectively.

A significant parameter in the valuation of the selectivity of the imprinted materials is the imprinting factor (IF) [28]. This is generally calculated as IF = KMIP/KNIP, where KMIP and KNIP are the linear slopes corresponding to MIP and NIP calibration curves, respectively. The IF of the detection system was 9.8 under the optimum conditions, which indicated the MIP@Si-CDs was much more selective than NIP@Si-CDs. Table 1 shows the comparison of the figure of merit the proposed method with other reported methods used for ACT quantification. As observed, the developed sensor has better sensitivity compared with some HPLC and GC methods [4,6,7], and also comparable sensitivity with ELISA method [8].

Compounds analogous to ACT such as imidacloprid, indoxacarb, chlorpyrifos, fenitrothion, dichlorvos, and difenoconazole were selected to assess the selectivity of the MIP@Si-CDs for ACT. In a typical test, 5.5 μM sample solutions were individually added to the solution and the fluorescence intensity was then measured. The selectivity of NIP@Si-CDs and Si-CDs were also studied and compared with those obtained via MIP@Si-CDs. A shown in Fig. 9 , the selectivity of MIP@Si-CDs for ACT detection is much higher than NIP@Si-CDs and Si-CDs because ACT interacts with recognition sites in the MIP@Si-CDs. However, there were no imprinted sites in the NIP@Si-CDs and Si-CDs. Thus, the proposed MIP sensor acts selectively in the determination of ACT in the presence of its analogues.

This work was aimed at the development of a simple, fast, and label-free fluorescence MIP@Si-CD nanocomposite for ACT determination. Si-CDs were prepared by hydrothermal method. The surface of the Si-CDs was then covered with MIP matrix via sol-gel polymerization. The novel fluorescent sensor is incorporated the unique properties of Si-CDs and excellent selectivity of MIP. Furthermore, this sensor has been successfully utilized in the quantification of the ACT in real samples.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are grateful to the Isfahan University of Technology, Iran (IUT) Research Council and Center of Excellence in Sensor and Green Chemistry for financial support.
